verses.text: God's anger burns against Israel, and David is moved to number the fighting men — a census springing from pride in his own strength. Even a king's impulse can be a snare. To count our forces in order to glory in them is to begin trusting the gift instead of the Giver.

God's anger burns against Israel, and David is moved to number the fighting men — a census springing from pride in his own strength. Even a king's impulse can be a snare. To count our forces in order to glory in them is to begin trusting the gift instead of the Giver.

Joab, of all people, objects — why should the king bring guilt on Israel? — but David overrules him. When even a hardened soldier sees the danger and we will not hear, we are far gone. Pride deafens us to the warnings God sends through unlikely mouths.

Joab, of all people, objects — why should the king bring guilt on Israel? — but David overrules him. When even a hardened soldier sees the danger and we will not hear, we are far gone. Pride deafens us to the warnings God sends through unlikely mouths.

For nine months the count goes on, and Joab brings back the totals — over a million men who draw the sword. The numbers are impressive, and that is just the problem. The figures meant to reassure David are the very evidence of the heart that trusted them.

For nine months the count goes on, and Joab brings back the totals — over a million men who draw the sword. The numbers are impressive, and that is just the problem. The figures meant to reassure David are the very evidence of the heart that trusted them.

When it is done, 'David's heart smote him,' and he confesses, 'I have sinned greatly... I have done very foolishly.' The conscience, once awakened, names the sin plainly. True repentance does not minimize or explain; it owns the folly and casts itself on God's mercy.

God offers three judgments through Gad, and David chooses to fall 'into the hand of the LORD; for his mercies are great: and let me not fall into the hand of man.' Faith reasons rightly even under the rod: better God's chastening than man's cruelty, for His mercy tempers all.

God offers three judgments through Gad, and David chooses to fall 'into the hand of the LORD; for his mercies are great: and let me not fall into the hand of man.' Faith reasons rightly even under the rod: better God's chastening than man's cruelty, for His mercy tempers all.

A plague kills seventy thousand, but as the angel stretches his hand over Jerusalem, the LORD relents: 'It is enough.' Judgment has its appointed limit in the heart of a merciful God. He does not afflict willingly, and stays His hand before the worst is done.

A plague kills seventy thousand, but as the angel stretches his hand over Jerusalem, the LORD relents: 'It is enough.' Judgment has its appointed limit in the heart of a merciful God. He does not afflict willingly, and stays His hand before the worst is done.

Seeing the angel, David cries, 'I have sinned... but these sheep, what have they done? let thine hand be against me.' The shepherd-king begs to bear the blow himself. Here is a faint shadow of the true Shepherd, who did stand between His flock and the stroke they deserved.

Gad tells David to build an altar on the threshingfloor of Araunah, the very spot where the angel stood. Where judgment was met, worship must rise. God turns the place of dread into the place of sacrifice — and this floor will become the site of the temple itself.

Gad tells David to build an altar on the threshingfloor of Araunah, the very spot where the angel stood. Where judgment was met, worship must rise. God turns the place of dread into the place of sacrifice — and this floor will become the site of the temple itself.

Araunah, seeing the king, offers the threshingfloor, the oxen, even the wood, all for free, as a gift to his lord. There is real generosity in the offer. Yet what is given to God is meant to be the giver's own, not gained without cost to ourselves.

Araunah, seeing the king, offers the threshingfloor, the oxen, even the wood, all for free, as a gift to his lord. There is real generosity in the offer. Yet what is given to God is meant to be the giver's own, not gained without cost to ourselves.

Araunah, seeing the king, offers the threshingfloor, the oxen, even the wood, all for free, as a gift to his lord. There is real generosity in the offer. Yet what is given to God is meant to be the giver's own, not gained without cost to ourselves.

David refuses: 'neither will I offer... unto the LORD my God of that which doth cost me nothing.' He pays the full price, builds the altar, and the plague is stayed. Worship that costs us nothing is worth nothing; true devotion gives God what is truly, dearly our own.
